Paper §1A: 'Gallium (GaAs/GaN wafers, RF chips, power electronics). GaAs/GaN wafers, RF chips, power electronics.' RF & Power Semiconductors ARE gallium-material devices; the paper explicitly names gallium as the semiconductor input for this device class. Value stands on that paper claim alone.

Paper §2D + §2B: ASML is the sole EUV maker and dominant DUV supplier; TSMC's leading-edge lithography is essentially single-sourced. Per-category value reflects share of TSMC's lithography-tool supply, not aggregate spend.
static.output_share_source_note: Paper §5 discusses ASML's revenue concentration on the leading-edge fab set; specific per-customer figure is industry-informed. TSMC is ASML's largest customer by a wide margin (EUV backlog is dominated by 3nm/2nm ramp). Estimate anchored to ASML's public revenue mix.

static.source_note: ASML is Samsung's sole EUV supplier; slight reduction vs TSMC reflects Samsung's greater share of DUV-only nodes (paper §2B: '3nm yields stuck at 30–40%').
static.output_share_source_note: Paper §5 (as above) — Samsung is ASML's #2 EUV buyer, meaningful share but well behind TSMC. Industry-informed estimate.

static.source_note: Intel needs EUV for leading-edge nodes (18A, 20A). ASML sole EUV maker. Industry-informed estimate of ASML's share of Intel's lithography-tool supply.
static.output_share_source_note: Paper §5 — Intel is ramping EUV for 18A/20A, growing share. Industry-informed estimate; smaller than Samsung today.

static.source_note: SK Hynix uses less EUV than TSMC/Samsung (leading-edge DRAM is starting to; HBM stacks less so). Industry-informed estimate of ASML's share of SK Hynix's lithography-tool supply.
Micron uses less EUV than SK Hynix/Samsung; largely DUV. Industry-informed estimate of ASML's share of Micron's lithography-tool supply.
Applied Materials is the dominant CVD / thin-film deposition supplier to leading-edge foundries. Value is industry-informed estimate of AMAT's share of TSMC's deposition-tool supply.
Memory + foundry mix raises AMAT's deposition share vs pure logic. Industry-informed estimate of AMAT's share of Samsung's deposition-tool supply.
AMAT dominant CVD supplier. Industry-informed estimate of AMAT's share of Intel's deposition-tool supply.
AMAT dominant CVD / thin-film supplier for memory. Industry-informed estimate of AMAT's share of SK Hynix's deposition-tool supply.
AMAT dominant CVD supplier for DRAM/NAND production. Industry-informed estimate of AMAT's share of Micron's deposition-tool supply.
Lam Research is the dominant etch supplier to leading-edge foundries. Value is industry-informed estimate of Lam's share of TSMC's etch-tool supply.
Lam dominant etch supplier; memory 3D stacks are etch-intensive. Industry-informed estimate of Lam's share of SK Hynix's etch-tool supply.
Lam dominant etch supplier for 3D memory. Industry-informed estimate of Lam's share of Micron's etch-tool supply.
Memory 3D-stack etch requirements raise Lam's share. Industry-informed estimate of Lam's share of Samsung's etch-tool supply.
KLA dominates process-control / inspection tools at leading-edge fabs. Value is industry-informed estimate of KLA's share of TSMC's inspection-tool supply.
Memory production requires denser metrology / inspection. Industry-informed estimate of KLA's share of Samsung's inspection-tool supply.
KLA dominant metrology / inspection at Intel. Industry-informed estimate.
KLA metrology / inspection for HBM production. Industry-informed estimate of KLA's share of SK Hynix's inspection-tool supply.

Tokyo Electron plays a secondary role in deposition / coating at leading-edge foundries (dominant in some specialty steps). Value is industry-informed estimate of TEL's share of TSMC's deposition-tool supply; bucket sums with AMAT.
TEL's coating / thin-film role in Samsung's mixed logic + memory operations. Industry-informed estimate; bucket sums with AMAT.
TEL's secondary deposition/coating role at SK Hynix. Industry-informed estimate; bucket sums with AMAT.

Paper §2B: TSMC has ~90% share at 3nm/2nm and is essentially the sole leading-edge foundry NVIDIA's advanced GPUs are fabbed on. Under per-category semantics this is NVIDIA's foundry-wafer supply share — restored from the cleanup-pass adjustment (TSMC 0.99 → 0.94) which was forced only by cross-category competition.
static.output_share_source_note: Paper §5 discusses TSMC's concentration on AI customers; NVIDIA is a top-3 TSMC customer (well behind Apple's ~25% but ahead of AMD, Broadcom, MediaTek in absolute terms). Industry-informed estimate from public TSMC customer mix.

static.source_note: TSMC is Broadcom's foundry. Value unchanged (0.95).
TSMC is AMD's foundry (7/5/3nm). Value unchanged (0.98) — under per-category this is AMD's foundry-wafer supply share.
Google TPU wafers via TSMC per paper §2G. Estimate.
AWS Trainium wafers are fabbed at TSMC per paper §2G. Paper does not quantify share of Amazon's total chip supply. Estimate.
Microsoft Maia wafers via TSMC per paper §2G. Estimate.
Meta MTIA wafers via TSMC per paper §2G. Estimate.
Samsung's small foundry role in NVIDIA's wafer mix (some prior-generation parts). Value unchanged (0.01) — paper does not quantify.

Paper §2C names HBM a co-equal bottleneck with the GPU. Paper does not quantify HBM's fraction of NVIDIA's BOM; industry-informed estimate: HBM ~25–35% of AI-GPU cost. NOTE — customer-concentration judgment (paper §5: SK Hynix ~90% NVIDIA-reliant) has been relocated to output_share on this edge, per the two-field schema.
AMD's Instinct GPUs similar HBM dependence; industry-informed.
Broadcom's ASIC customers use HBM; industry-informed, lower share than NVIDIA/AMD given non-HBM parts of Broadcom's business.

Under per-category semantics, this is SK Hynix's share of NVIDIA's memory supply (NOT of aggregate supplies). SK Hynix is NVIDIA's dominant HBM supplier per paper §5; industry-informed estimate ~60% of NVIDIA's memory. Bucket incomplete: Samsung/Micron memory paths into NVIDIA are not modelled as direct edges.
static.output_share_source_note: Paper §5 (Financial Interdependencies): 'SK Hynix ~90% reliant on NVIDIA for HBM.' This is the paper's own figure on the paper's own edge — the previous placement on e:hbm-input-nvidia asserted the ratio for the aggregate HBM product, which overstated the paper (Micron and Samsung sell elsewhere).
